items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Once again I’m tormented
by vague misgivings...
Shpak’s missing a tape recorder,
the ambassador’s missing a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: This segment is essentially perfect. The translation preserves the mock-heroic, high-brow phrasing (“tormented by vague misgivings”), which is the core comedic engine. It also correctly avoids the wrong possessive reading (“Shpak has...”), instead conveying loss (“Shpak’s missing... the ambassador’s missing...”), matching the reference note that both items are gone. Rhythm and parallelism are maintained well for a subtitle line.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—very pleased.
Tsar—very pleased. Tsar.
Very pleased. Hello, Tsar.

Tsar—very pleased.
Hello, Tsar! Very pleased!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation successfully reproduces the joke: repetitive, banal politeness and self-introduction reduced to a Soviet-style social tic, made absurd by the royal context. The repetition and fragmentation are preserved, and the register stays mundanely polite rather than archaic—good. Minor point: the reference’s “I’m the Tsar” makes the self-introduction aspect slightly clearer; the provided version relies on vocative “Tsar” which can read as addressing rather than introducing, though the repetition still signals the intended gag.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Perfect: same force, same pompous performative demand, and it fits subtitle brevity. The comedic character shift (uptight bureaucrat suddenly speaking like an autocrat) is fully preserved.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman—I'm in mourning.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: The line largely works: dismissive address + elevated melancholy in a mundane setting. However, “in mourning” is a semantic shift: it implies bereavement/ritual mourning rather than generalized sorrow/печаль. That can slightly miscolor the Tsar’s archaic, lofty sadness and the humor of overblown melancholy. “I am in sorrow” / “I’m in grief” would be closer while still high-register.

items.translation:
Bricked in!
Bricked in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Mostly successful: the core misunderstanding (being “walled in” by demons) is preserved, and “the demons” correctly functions as the agent. The main weakness is stylistic/idiomatic: “Bricked in!” is less natural than “They’ve walled me in!” and loses the plaintive, accusatory cadence of the repeated passive construction. Still comprehensible and funny, but slightly rough.

items.translation: So that’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Good rendering of the religious exclamation and causal attribution of a “miracle.” It keeps the archaic-religious concept “life-giving cross.” The only minor loss is rhetorical grandeur: “Behold the power…” is more proclamatory; “So that’s what… can do” is slightly more conversational/modern, softening the Tsar’s certainty and the mock-solemnity. Still works in context.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Meaning is conveyed, but the creative effect is weakened. The Russian is a set, emcee-like command (“All dance!”) with a showman/MC flavor; the reference solution (“The floor is open to everyone!”) captures that public-announcement vibe and the comic ‘event’ framing. “Everybody dance!” is understandable but more generic and less characterful as a catchphrase, reducing the meme-like punch.

items.translation: I picked a good time to drop by!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Strong and idiomatic. It preserves the breezy self-satisfaction and opportunistic timing that makes Miloslavsky likable. “Drop by” fits the casual criminal cheerfulness. No meaningful loss of humor.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Overseas “caviar”—eggplant spread!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: This is very good and aligns with the reference’s key warning: do not call eggplant spread “caviar” straightforwardly. The translator smartly uses scare quotes around “caviar” and then clarifies “eggplant spread,” preserving the Soviet-food satire and the punchline inversion. Rhythm and escalation (“and yes!”) are kept.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
with backbreaking labor—

it’s all gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The comedic inventory and the escalating “three” pattern are preserved well, including the slightly disjointed afterthought quality (“domestic... suede jacket—three”), which is important to the joke. The only minor nuance: the reference analysis notes he forgets to increment the suede jackets at first; this translation already makes it “suede jacket—three,” which is faithful to the line as given here, so no issue. Overall effective and meme-consistent.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And you’ll be cured!

And you will be cured too!

And I will be cured too!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: The repetition and resigned comic logic are preserved. However, shifting from impersonal “they’ll cure…” (evoking the ominous external institution—psychiatric ward) to “you’ll be cured” slightly blunts the social subtext and the darkly comic inevitability. Also, “you will be cured” is a bit stiffer than the surrounding colloquial tone. Still works, but loses a layer of the joke described in the reference analysis.

summary.interpretation: 0.64 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.13 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
